questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the Delaware Real Estate Guaranty Fund?
questions.correct_answer: Protect the public from licensee misconduct
distractors: Insure licensees against client lawsuits
Fund Commission operations and staff
Cover property damage during transactions
questions.explanation: The Guaranty Fund exists to compensate members of the public who suffer financial loss due to the misconduct of a licensed real estate professional. It does not insure licensees, fund operations, or cover property damage.

questions.question_text: A buyer loses $40,000 due to a licensee's fraud. What is the maximum she can recover from the Guaranty Fund?
questions.explanation: As amended in 2026, the Guaranty Fund pays up to $50,000 in the aggregate per transaction or claim, regardless of the number of persons aggrieved (24 Del. C. §2922(a)). Because the buyer's unpaid judgment loss of $40,000 is below the $50,000 cap, she may recover the full $40,000, subject to the Commission's order after a hearing.

questions.question_text: Before filing a Guaranty Fund claim, what must a claimant first obtain?
questions.correct_answer: A final court judgment against the licensee
distractors: A Commission ruling against the licensee
A demand letter from the licensee's broker
A police report documenting the fraud
questions.explanation: Delaware requires claimants to first obtain a final court judgment and then demonstrate they cannot collect from the licensee before the Guaranty Fund will pay. This ensures the fund is a last resort after all other collection methods are exhausted.

questions.question_text: The Guaranty Fund balance drops to $200,000. What action will the Commission take?
questions.correct_answer: Assess licensees on a pro rata basis
distractors: Suspend all fund claims until recovery
Request a state general fund allocation
Increase renewal fees for the next cycle
questions.explanation: The Commission must maintain the Fund above $350,000 (24 Del. C. §2922(i), as amended 2026). If the balance falls below $250,000, the Commission assesses each licensee a pro rata fee at the next license renewal date sufficient to return the Fund to the $250,000 level (§2922(j)). At $200,000 the fund is below the statutory assessment trigger.

questions.question_text: Which claimant is expressly barred from recovering from the Guaranty Fund?
questions.correct_answer: The spouse of the licensee against whom judgment was entered
distractors: A buyer who obtained a final judgment against the licensee
A client of the licensee's brokerage
An out-of-state purchaser of Delaware property
questions.explanation: The Guaranty Fund is open to any person who obtains a final judgment against a licensee or brokerage organization for theft, forgery, fraud, misrepresentation, or deceit. The claimant must show she is not the spouse of the judgment debtor (or the spouse's personal representative), must file a verified claim within 60 days of final judgment, and must show diligent pursuit and exhaustion of all reasonable collection remedies (24 Del. C. §2922(b)).

questions.question_text: The Guaranty Fund pays $25,000 on a claim against a salesperson. What happens to her license?
questions.correct_answer: The Commission may suspend or revoke it
distractors: It is automatically suspended without any Commission action
Nothing — a fund payment has no licensure consequence
It is permanently revoked by operation of law
questions.explanation: When the Fund pays a claim on a licensee's account, the Commission may suspend or revoke that licensee's license, and in the Commission's discretion the licensee is ineligible for a new license until the Fund is repaid in full plus interest at the legal rate (24 Del. C. §2922(d)). A bankruptcy discharge does not relieve the licensee of these consequences.

questions.question_text: A licensee lost her license after a Guaranty Fund payout and wants a new license. What must she do first?
questions.correct_answer: Repay the fund in full, plus interest
distractors: Complete 21 hours of continuing education
Wait two years and reapply for license
Pass both portions of the licensing exam
questions.explanation: Under 24 Del. C. §2922(d), a licensee whose misconduct caused a Fund payout is, in the Commission's discretion, ineligible for a new license until the amount paid from the Fund is repaid in full plus interest at the legal rate. A discharge in bankruptcy does not relieve the licensee of this repayment condition.

questions.question_text: Which types of misconduct does the Guaranty Fund cover?
distractors: Any violation of Commission regulations
Only criminal acts resulting in conviction
Negligence and breach of fiduciary duty
questions.explanation: The fund specifically covers theft, forgery, fraud, misrepresentation, and deceit by a licensee or the licensee's unlicensed employee. General negligence or minor rule violations are not covered — the misconduct must involve intentional wrongdoing or deception.

questions.question_text: A licensee defrauds a client while acting in a personal capacity, not as an agent. Is the loss covered by the Guaranty Fund?
questions.correct_answer: No, acts must be in a licensed capacity
distractors: Yes, any licensee misconduct qualifies
Yes, if the loss exceeds $5,000
Only if Commission finds the licensee guilty
questions.explanation: The Guaranty Fund only covers losses from acts performed in a licensee's professional capacity. Personal transactions or activities unrelated to real estate practice are not covered, even if the wrongdoer holds an active license.

questions.question_text: A licensee's unlicensed assistant embezzles a client's earnest money deposit. Is this covered by the Guaranty Fund?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, unlicensed employee acts are covered
distractors: No, only licensed agents are covered
No, the client must sue the assistant
Only if the broker was aware of the theft
questions.explanation: The Guaranty Fund covers misconduct by licensees and their unlicensed employees acting within the scope of the real estate business. The client can pursue a Guaranty Fund claim even though the person who committed the theft was not personally licensed.
